# Markdown Rendering & Analysis Extraction Improvements

## Overview
This PR enhances the markdown rendering capabilities across the frontend and PDF generation, improves analysis extraction robustness, and refines the clinical analysis prompts for better specialist-level insights.

## Changes

### Frontend Markdown Rendering (`frontend/src/components/AnalysisResults.tsx`)
- **Added `renderMarkdown()` function** to convert markdown bold syntax (`**text**`) to HTML `<strong>` tags
- Applied markdown rendering to:
  - Executive Summary
  - Key Findings list items
  - Recommendations list items
- Ensures bold text displays correctly in the web UI instead of showing literal `**` markers

### Backend Analysis Extraction (`backend/src/handlers/analysis.ts`)
- **Enhanced extraction logic** with fallback mechanisms:
  - Primary extraction uses section-based parsing for structured content
  - Fallback extraction searches full text for numbered/bulleted lists when section extra

pull_requests.title: Feature/initial mock
pull_requests.body:
# Implement Local Development Environment with Ollama and LocalStack

## Summary

This PR implements a complete local development environment for HealthWeave that enables document analysis using free, local AI models (Ollama) and AWS service emulation (LocalStack). The system now supports full end-to-end document upload, text extraction, AI analysis, and PDF report generation without requiring AWS credentials or API costs.

## What's New

### 🎯 Core Features
- **Local AI Analysis**: Integrated Ollama (llama3.2) as a free, local LLM alternative to AWS Bedrock
- **PDF Text Extraction**: Implemented `pdfjs-dist` for extracting text from PDF documents
- **Multi-tier Fallback System**: Bedrock → Anthropic API → Ollama (automatic fallback chain)
- **PDF Report Generation**: Full PDF report download with structured analysis results
- **Error Handling**: Improved error handling and logging throughout the system

### 🛠️ Technical Implementation

#### AI Service Integration (`ba

issues.title: ✅ [FASE 3.3] Implementar integração com Google Calendar (mock)

issues.title: 🔜 [FASE 5.4] Realizar testes com usuários reais (beta)

issues.title: 🔜 [FASE 5.3] Configurar variáveis de ambiente e segurança
issues.body: Separar tokens e segredos sensíveis em .env, configurando corretamente nos ambientes locais e em produção. Usar python-dotenv ou equivalente.

issues.title: 🔜 [FASE 4.4] Implementar agendamento com confirmação de horário
issues.body: Confirmar o horário escolhido pelo paciente e criar um evento no Google Calendar com os dados da consulta. Enviar mensagem final com link do evento.

issues.title: 🔜 [FASE 4.3] Implementar leitura e exibição de horários disponíveis
issues.body: Consultar a agenda do Google Calendar e retornar para o paciente os horários disponíveis para atendimento (evitando sobreposição).

issues.title: 🔜 [FASE 4.2] Implementar verificação de convênios aceitos
issues.body: Checar se o convênio informado pelo paciente está entre os aceitos. Em caso negativo, oferecer opções particulares com preço.

issues.title: 🔜 [FASE 4.1] Implementar fluxo de identificação de tipo de paciente
issues.body: Analisar mensagens e identificar se o paciente quer atendimento particular ou por convênio. Salvar essa escolha para uso posterior no fluxo.

issues.title: ✅ [FASE 3.1] Implementar estrutura inicial de rotas e handlers com FastAPI
issues.body: Criar a estrutura básica com FastAPI, com rotas organizadas para os fluxos principais (ex: /whatsapp, /agenda, /openai). Incluir sistema de respostas padronizadas e status HTTP claros.

issues.title: [FASE 2.4] Planejar integração com APIs
issues.body: Especificar e documentar como será feita a autenticação e o uso das APIs do WhatsApp Business, Google Calendar e OpenAI. Definir tokens, escopos, callbacks e simular chamadas (mock).

issues.title: [FASE 2.3] Estruturar arquitetura do sistema

pull_requests.title: Otimização de Agendamento para a Clínica Médica
pull_requests.body:
## Descrição
Este PR implementa o novo sistema de agendamento conforme requisitado, com horários específicos para cada dia da semana:
- Segunda/Quarta/Sexta: 14:00-17:45 (5 slots de 45 minutos)
- Terça/Quinta/Sábado: 8:30-12:15 (5 slots de 45 minutos)

Além disso, corrige problemas no algoritmo de otimização de slots e melhora os testes unitários.

## Mudanças principais:
- Criação da classe `ClinicSettings` com configurações da clínica
- Implementação do sistema de pontuação para ordenação de slots preferenciais
- Remoção da lógica de horário de almoço/rush conforme solicitado
- Correção dos testes unitários e uso de mocks apropriados
- Desabilitação temporária de testes do Google Calendar (marcados com skip)

## Impacto técnico:
- Modificação do algoritmo de priorização de slots
- Atualização do serviço de calendário para usar as novas configurações
- Organização de testes para melhor manutenção

## Testes realizados:
- Testes unitários do algoritmo de otimização

pull_requests.title: Implementação do SimplifiedSlotService com testes e API
pull_requests.body:
Este PR implementa um novo serviço simplificado para gerenciamento de slots de consulta com persistência em JSON.

### Principais adições:
- Novo serviço `SimplifiedSlotService` para gerenciamento de slots de agendamento baseado em arquivo JSON
- Configuração de horários da clínica via arquivo JSON (`app/config/clinic_schedule.json`)
- API endpoints REST para interagir com o serviço em `/api/simplified-slots/`
- Testes abrangentes para validar o funcionamento do serviço
- Documentação atualizada no README sobre a configuração de horários da clínica

### Benefícios:
- Solução leve e simples para clínicas de pequeno porte
- Configuração flexível de dias e horários de atendimento
- Persistência em JSON sem necessidade de banco de dados
- API REST completa para integração com frontends

pull_requests.title: feat: Implementa geração e gerenciamento de slots de agendamento com …
pull_requests.body:
## Descrição

Esta PR implementa um sistema completo de geração e gerenciamento de slots para agendamentos, com persistência em banco de dados SQLite.

## Funcionalidades Adicionadas:

- **SlotService**: Serviço em memória para gerenciar slots de horário
  - Criação de slots individuais
  - Geração automática de múltiplos slots baseados em datas, horários e duração configuráveis
  - Suporte a exclusão de dias específicos (ex: finais de semana)
  - Métodos para reservar, liberar e associar slots a agendamentos
  
- **SlotManagementService**: Serviço para persistência dos slots
  - Armazenamento dos slots em banco de dados SQLite
  - Inicialização automática do banco e criação de tabelas
  - Métodos para consultar slots disponíveis em períodos específicos
  - Limpeza automática de slots vencidos

- **API RESTful**: Endpoints para interação com o sistema de slots
  - POST `/slots/generate`: Gera slots para um período configurável
  - GET `/slots/available`: Lista slots

        *   Calls `create_calendar_event` after successful internal slot reservation.
        *   Sends final confirmation message and sets state to `COMPLETED` on successful event creation.
        *   Calls `scheduling_service.cancel_appointment` to free the slot if event creation fails.
        *   Sets state to `HUMAN_TAKEOVER` on event creation failure.

pull_requests.title: feat: Add patient data collection and calendar integration logic
pull_requests.body:
This PR introduces the collection of patient details (name, date of birth) within the WhatsApp conversation flow and integrates the logic for attempting Google Calendar event creation upon successful slot selection.

**Changes:**

*   **WhatsApp API (`app/api/whatsapp.py`):**
    *   Reintroduces `AWAITING_NAME` and adds `AWAITING_DOB` states after insurance validation to collect patient's full name and date of birth.
    *   Updates conversation state transitions accordingly.
    *   Modifies the `AWAITING_SLOT_CHOICE` handler:
        *   After successful internal slot reservation, it now gathers patient info (name, dob, phone, insurance) and slot details (start/end times).
        *   Includes placeholder logic to call a `create_calendar_event` function (to be implemented in a Calendar service).
        *   If calendar event creation succeeds (placeholder currently assumes success):
            *   Sends a final confirmation message to the user via WhatsApp.

    - Boas-vindas e escolha inicial (link vs chat).
    - Diferenciação entre atendimento particular e por convênio.
    - Validação de nome de convênio.
    - Solicitação de documentos para convênio.

    - Transição para HUMAN_TAKEOVER nos casos apropriados (particular, falha sem mais slots).
- Adiciona testes de API em tests/api/test_whatsapp_api.py para verificar os diferentes estados e transições.
- Corrige ImportError em app/main.py removendo referências à API de ChatGPT deletada.
- Corrige configuração do pytest-asyncio em pyproject.toml e atualiza versão para 0.21.2 para compatibilidade com pytest 8.2.
- Resolve falhas nos testes relacionadas ao mock de variáveis de ambiente e adiciona await em chamada assíncrona.

pull_requests.title: Feat: Integrate Meta WhatsApp Cloud API for Sending Messages
pull_requests.body:
This PR implements the functionality to send messages using the official Meta WhatsApp Cloud API, replacing the previous mock implementation.

**Key Changes:**

**1. app/services/whatsapp_service.py:**

- [x] Added httpx for making HTTP requests.
- [x] Included loading of WHATSAPP_PHONE_NUMBER_ID from environment variables alongside the WHATSAPP_API_TOKEN.
- [x] Implemented the send_message method to make a POST request to the Meta Graph API endpoint (https://graph.facebook.com/v19.0/{phone_number_id}/messages).
- [x] Constructed the required Authorization: Bearer header and the JSON payload according to Meta API specifications for sending text messages.
- [x] Added robust error handling using response.raise_for_status() and try...except blocks for httpx.RequestError, httpx.HTTPStatusError, and general exceptions.

**tests/services/test_whatsapp_service.py:**

- [x] Refactored tests for send_message and send_appointment_confirmation to use @patch('httpx.Client.post').
-

pull_requests.title: Feat: Implement Real OpenAI API Calls in ChatGPTService
pull_requests.body:
This PR finalizes the ChatGPT integration by:

1.  Updating `ChatGPTService` to use the actual `openai` client and make real API calls to `chat.completions.create`.
2.  Explicitly passing the API key during client initialization within the service.
3.  Refactoring `tests/services/test_chatgpt_service.py`:
    *   Removed mocking of the entire `openai.OpenAI` client initialization.
    *   Updated fixtures to initialize the real `ChatGPTService`.
    *   Patched the specific `client.chat.completions.create` method on the service's client instance to isolate tests from actual network calls.
    *   Ensured all tests for `ChatGPTService` pass with the new mocking strategy.

This completes a core functionality step, moving away from the previous mock implementation and preparing for real-world usage and further integration testing.

pull_requests.title: 5.1 testes unitarios integracao
pull_requests.body:
This Pull Request addresses significant challenges encountered during the implementation and testing of asynchronous operations, primarily related to pytest-asyncio fixture handling, dependency conflicts, and mocking complexities.
After numerous attempts to resolve these issues (including version changes, configuration adjustments in pyproject.toml, and test refactoring), the decision was made to simplify the current architecture by converting the core services and their corresponding tests to a synchronous model.

Key Changes:

Synchronous Refactoring:
Removed async / await keywords from methods in SlotService, SchedulingService, ChatGPTService, WhatsAppService, and AppointmentOrchestrator.

Updated all corresponding tests in tests/services/, tests/integration/, and tests/ root to remove async markers, asyncio.run calls, and replaced AsyncMock with MagicMock.

Dependency Cleanup:
Removed pytest-asyncio, anyio, and sniffio from requirements.txt as they are no longer direct

issues.title: Release Clinical-Grade Blood Pressure Prediction Ensemble Model on Hugging Face
issues.body:
Hi @mdbasit897 🤗

I'm Niels and work as part of the open-source team at Hugging Face. I discovered your work on Arxiv and was wondering whether you would like to submit it to [hf.co/papers](https://hf.co/papers) to improve its discoverability.If you are one of the authors, you can submit it at https://huggingface.co/papers/submit.

The paper page lets people discuss about your paper and lets them find artifacts about it (your models for instance),
you can also claim the paper as yours which will show up on your public profile at HF, add Github and project page URLs.

Would you like to host the trained model artifacts you've released on https://huggingface.co/models?
I saw that your GitHub repository `mdbasit897/clinical-bp-prediction-ehr` mentions "Trained model artifacts for reuse: - Gradient boosting ensemble - Imputation strategy pickles - Feature processor configurations" under the "models/" output directory. Hosting these models on Hugging Face will give you more visibility/enable

issues.title: Issue with Degrading Text Generation Quality in Fine-Tuning BioGPT
issues.body:
Good afternoon!
Thank you for open-sourcing such fantastic work!
I have been trying to fine-tune bioGPT on a subset of textual data to provide more knowledge on some specific domain.
However, when I am training the model for one epoch on a very small subset of PubMed abstracts, the model loses the ability to generate comprehensive English and seems to output just random words. Would you be able to provide insights into why the model degrades so fast?

I am fine-tuning the base pre-trained model from https://huggingface.co/microsoft/biogpt:

```
from transformers import BioGptTokenizer, BioGptForCausalLM

abs_tokenizer = BioGptTokenizer.from_pretrained("microsoft/biogpt")
abs_model = BioGptForCausalLM.from_pretrained("microsoft/biogpt")
```

To fine-tune, I am freezing all layers besides the last one and feeding new data into self-supervised training to predict the next word based on the previous:

```

## Freeze embed_tokens and embed_positions
for param in abs_model

pull_requests.title: Bump torch from 1.12.0 to 2.2.0
pull_requests.body:
Bumps [torch](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch) from 1.12.0 to 2.2.0.
Release notes

Sourced from torch's releases.

PyTorch 2.2: FlashAttention-v2, AOTInductor

PyTorch 2.2 Release Notes

  • Highlights
  • Backwards Incompatible Changes
  • Deprecations
  • New Features
  • Improvements
  • Bug fixes
  • Performance
  • Documentation

Highlights

We are excited to announce the release of PyTorch® 2.2! PyTorch 2.2 offers ~2x performance improvements to scaled_dot_product_attention via FlashAttention-v2 integration, as well as AOTInductor, a new ahead-of-time compilation and deployment tool built for non-python server-side deployments.

This release also includes improved torch.compile support for Optimizers, a number of new inductor optimizations, and a new logging mechanism

We're happy to announce the 1.5.0 release.

You can read the release highlights under https://scikit-learn.org/stable/auto_examples/release_highlights/plot_release_highlights_1_5_0.html and the long version of the change log under https://scikit-learn.org/stable/whats_new/v1.5.html

This version supports Python versions 3.9 to 3.12.

You can upgrade with pip as usual:

pip install -U scikit-learn

The conda-forge builds can be installed using:

conda instal

issues.title: `docker-compose up` Fails: .env File Missing in Functions Directory
issues.body: **Describe:** When running `docker-compose up` in the `functions` directory, it fails due to the missing `.env` file. The specific information is as follows: **Repro Steps:** 1. Run `cd functions` 2. Run `docker-compose up` **Environment:** - OS: Windows and Linux - Azd version: azd version 1.22.5 (commit f1850eb726d560d54118b4fa1c5f770d5f38e7f5). **Expected behavior:** `docker-compose up` can run successfully. @sashimono-san and @hemarina for notification.

issues.title: Windows Environment Variable Setting Steps Missing in `Backend Setup` Section
issues.body: **Describe:** The README only provides `Linux/macOS` commands for setting environment variables using `export` in [Backend Setup](https://github.com/microsoft/healthcare-ai-model-evaluator/tree/main?tab=readme-ov-file#backend-setup) Section, but these commands do not work on `Windows`. Please add equivalent instructions for setting environment variables in `Windows` environments. **Suggestion:** Add the following content: ``` # For PowerShell $env:AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING="Your Storage Account connection string" $env:COSMOSDB_CONNECTION_STRING="Your mongodb connection string" ``` @sashimono-san and @hemarina for notification.

issues.title: `azd up` fails on both Windows and Linux.
issues.body: **Describe:** Running the `azd up` command on both `Windows` and` Linux` failed, with the following error messages: - For `Windows`: The current `azure.yaml` file's [prebuild](https://github.com/microsoft/healthcare-ai-model-evaluator/blob/main/azure.yaml#L12) hook is only configured for the `posix` platform, and lacks configuration for the **Windows** platform. Similarly, other hooks like [prepackage](https://github.com/microsoft/healthcare-ai-model-evaluator/blob/main/azure.yaml#L41) and [postprovision](https://github.com/microsoft/healthcare-ai-model-evaluator/blob/main/azure.yaml#L47) are also missing configurations for `Windows`. - For `posix`: The `prebuild` hook failed because the [../infra/scripts/update-env.sh](https://github.com/microsoft/healthcare-ai-model-evaluator/blob/main/infra/scripts/update-env.sh#L6-L8) script uses `azd env get-value` t

issues.title: Missing the step for cloning or initializing the project (azd init) in README
issues.body: **Describe:** The README does not include the command `azd init -t microsoft/healthcare-ai-model-evaluator` for cloning or initializing the project. As an azd project, it is recommended to add `azd init -t microsoft/healthcare-ai-model-evaluator` to the README so that users can directly initialize the project using the azd tool. **Suggestion:** Add the step `azd init -t microsoft/healthcare-ai-model-evaluator` to the README. @sashimono-san and @hemarina for notification.

pull_requests.title: Document .env file requirement for docker-compose
pull_requests.body:
`docker-compose up` in functions directory fails because `docker-compose.yml` references `.env` (required by `haime-evaluator` service), but only `.env.example` exists in the repository.

## Changes

Added explicit `.env` creation step to all documentation locations that mention `docker-compose up`:

- **functions/README.md**: Added IMPORTANT callout in Step 2 emphasizing the requirement
- **README.md**: Added creation step with inline comments in Functions Setup section  
- **DEPLOYMENT.md**: Added creation step in Local Development section

## Example

```bash
cd functions
# Create .env file from template (required for docker-compose)
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env with your Azure OpenAI credentials if needed

docker-compose up
```

The `.env.example` contains defaults for local development with Azurite. Users only need to update API keys if testing against real Azure services.

pull_requests.title: Add Windows PowerShell environment variable setup instructions
pull_requests.body:
README Backend Setup section only documented `export` syntax for Linux/macOS, leaving Windows users unable to configure required environment variables.

## Changes

- Added PowerShell `$env:` syntax for setting environment variables on Windows
- Added platform clarifying comments to distinguish Linux/macOS vs PowerShell instructions

```bash
# For Linux/macOS
export AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING=[Your Storage Account connection string]
export COSMOSDB_CONNECTION_STRING=[Your mongodb connection string]

# For PowerShell
$env:AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING="Your Storage Account connection string"
$env:COSMOSDB_CONNECTION_STRING="Your mongodb connection string"
```

pull_requests.title: Replace Console.WriteLine with ILogger in Program.cs
Fix middleware ordering in Program.cs to follow ASP.NET Core best practices
Replace Console.WriteLine with ILogger in Program.cs
pull_requests.body:
Removes debugging Console.WriteLine statements left in production code, replacing them with proper logging infrastructure.

### Changes
- Added `ILogger<Program>` instance after app build for startup configuration logging
- Replaced all Console.WriteLine calls with appropriate log levels:
  - `LogInformation` for setup paths, directory checks, and redirects
  - `LogDebug` for static file middleware and React routing details
  - `LogError` for MongoDB connection failures with exception object
- Used structured logging with named parameters instead of string interpolation

### Example
```csharp
// Before
Console.WriteLine($"Setting up static files for webapp at: {webappPath}");

// After
logger.LogInformation("Setting up static files for webapp at: {WebappPath}", webappPath);
```

Addresses feedback from #9.

Release v4.53.0

Gemma3n

Gemma 3n models are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices. They are capable of multimodal input, handling text, image, video, and audio input, and generating text outputs, with open weights for pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants. These models were trained with data in over 140 spoken languages.

Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements. This technique allows the models to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters, which is lower than the total number of parameters they contain. For more information on Gemma 3n's efficient parameter management technology, see the

issues.title: NaN values in Performer attention layer seems to cause training failure during fine-tuning in the first epoch
issues.body:
## Description

During fine-tuning with the PFT (Partial Fine-Tuning) mode, the model produces NaN values in predictions, causing training to fail with a `ValueError: Input contains NaN` error in the very first iteration. The issue could possibly originate from numerical instability in the Performer attention mechanism's `softmax_kernel` function.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Set up the environment using COMPASS `requirements.txt`
2. Load the pre-trained model and use `FineTuner.tune_with_test()` method from `compass/main.py` with PFT mode on leave-one-out cross-validation setup
3. Training fails immediately in the first epoch during evaluation

pull_requests.title: Extend GPT-OSS-20B tutorial into full integration into starter kit
pull_requests.body:
## Overview

This PR extends the basic GPT-OSS-20B tutorial (PR #14) with **full-featured support** for OpenAI’s GPT-OSS-20B (paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.10925, Hugging Face: https://huggingface.co/openai/gpt-oss-20b) in the CUREBench starter kit.

GPT-OSS-20B is a state-of-the-art **open-weight reasoning model** designed for both **internal reasoning** and **agentic tool use** via the Harmony response format.

This PR enables participants to explore GPT-OSS-20B across **both competition tracks**:

* **Track 1 (Internal Reasoning):** chain-of-thought style reasoning without tools.
* **Track 2 (Agentic Tool Use):** tool-augmented workflows with built-in tools (`python`, etc.) or biomedical tools from ToolUniverse (https://github.com/mims-harvard/ToolUniverse).

---

### Relation to PR #14

**This PR extends and complements** PR #14

**PR #14 (Main Tutorial PR)**

* A lightweight update: added a tutorial + README example showing participants how to call `openai/g

pull_requests.title: Add GPT-OSS-20B Tutorial and README Update for CUREBench Participants
pull_requests.body:
## Overview

This PR introduces a simple, participant-facing guide for using OpenAI’s GPT-OSS-20B (paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.10925, Hugging Face: https://huggingface.co/openai/gpt-oss-20b) in the CUREBench competition. GPT-OSS-20B is an **open-weight reasoning model** (Apache 2.0 license) that participants can run locally or fine-tune for improved performance.

The changes provide clear, step-by-step instructions for both **zero-shot usage** and **fine-tuned checkpoints**, lowering the barrier for participants who want to experiment with open-weight models.

---

## Key Changes

### 1. Updated `README.md`

* Added instructions for running GPT-OSS-20B alongside existing API and open-weight models.
* Expanded metadata configuration examples to include **GPT-OSS-20B** for both zero-shot and fine-tuned cases.
* Clarified hardware requirements and quantization options (FP16 vs MXFP4 vs 8-bit).

### 2. New Tutorial

* Added **`tutorials/tutorial_gptoss20b.md`**:

pull_requests.title: The first column must be moved into the index
pull_requests.body:
Why: 

In the section `Reading in ClinVec embeddings into Python` you do `df['node_index'] = df.index` but `df.index` by default takes the values between 0 and length of the array. 

The first column of the CSV stores the actual node indices, so that must be moved into the df index instead.

We are excited to announce the release of PyTorch® 2.2! PyTorch 2.2 offers ~2x performance improvements to scaled_dot_product_attention via FlashAttention-v2 integration, as well as AOTInductor, a new ahead-of-time compilation and deployment tool built for non-python server-side deployments.

Hi,

So I wonder if I made mistakes about the hyperparameters.   I followed the hyperparameters in your paper and your code:

```
    epochs = 200,
    patience = 10,
    lr = 0.01,
    weight_decay = 5e-4,
    hidden = 16,
    dropout = 0.5,
    modelname = 'GIN',
    GNNGuard = True,
    seed = 15,
```

Could you take a look and see which values of hyperparameters should I use?  Thanks.


P.S. To save memory, I changed `cosine_s

issues.title: Question upon running dataset ogbn-arxiv
issues.body:
Hi Xiang,

Thanks for your impressive work!
I have a question regarding to running ogbn-arxiv dataset. It seems like directly running this large dataset under the current framework within a single GPU is not possible. Could you provide any tips on efficiently conduct robust evaluation on ogbn-arxiv? Specifically, I have several questions such as:

- Do you use any sampling techniques during training GNN models? If it is, what sampling method would you recommend?
- Do you run ogbn-arxiv on a single GPU or CPU? If it can be done on a single GPU, how large memories should it has?
- It seems like it would take weeks for directly employing Mettack and Nettack to finish attacking on such large network, if it is the case it would be very infeasible especially for the poisoning setting. I am not sure if this observation is correct.

Thanks in advance!

issues.title: Possible bugs in pg_explainer.py
issues.body:
Thank you again for your great work!
However, I encountered some confusing coding snippets in your implementation in the script, GraphXAI/graphxai/explainers/pg_explainer.py (Lines 119-123):

            if self.explain_graph: 
                h = torch.cat([h1, h2], dim=1)
            else:
                h3 = emb.repeat(h1.shape[0], 1)
                h = torch.cat([h1, h2], dim=1)

I believe that "self.explain_graph = True" corresponds to graph-level explanation, while "self.explain_graph = False" corresponds to node-level explanation. However, in your implementation (I know that you adapted this from the DIG library), the `h3 = emb.repeat(h1.shape[0], 1)` is defined but never used. So how to distinguish graph-level explanation and node-level explanation?

issues.title: Bug in code for PGMExplainer
issues.body:
I believe there is a significant error for PGMExplainer.

The comments for function 'PGM_perturb_node_features' (in GraphXAI/graphxai/utils/perturb/perturb.py) state that it should return:

        x_pert (torch.Tensor, [n x d]): perturbed feature matrix
        node_mask (torch.Tensor, [n]): Boolean mask of perturbed nodes

However:
1. It returns:   x, pert_mask   
   where x which is the input to the function, rather than the perturbed values
2. It actually should be returning: x_new and pert_mask

  pip install [options] [-e] <vcs project url> ...

```
┌────────────┬──────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│    code    │  med_code_node_mapping[:4]   │                        mt."desc"[:60]                        │
│  varchar   │           int64[]            │                           varchar                            │
├────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ icd9:00.25 │ [63160, 39991]               │ Intravascular Imaging of Renal Vessels - This diagnostic pro │
│ icd9:00.28 │ [63160, 39991]               │ Intravascular Imaging of Other Specified Vessels - This proc │
│ icd9:00.29 │ [63160, 39991]               │ Paratyphoid Fever, Unspecified - A bacterial infection cause │
```

ICD-9 00.29 is actually "Intravascular imaging, unspecified vessel(s)". The present description is incorrectly taken from 002.9.

In ICD-9, codes like NN.xxx are procedures and N

issues.title: 88 duplicate nodes with conflicting names
issues.body:
We have observed that 88 nodes in the July 2023 version of the KG (without taking the LCC) contain non-harmonized node names. For example, the same gene MT-ND5 is represented as either "ND5" or "MT-ND5," and the gallbladder anatomy node is represented as either "gallbladder" or "gall bladder." Below is a quick R script to merge these nodes:

```R
# load edges of updated PrimeKG
primeKG_edges = fread(here(primeKG_dir, "kg", "auxiliary", "kg_raw.csv"))

# replace "off-label use" with off_label_use
primeKG_edges[relation == "off-label use", relation := "off_label_use"]

# construct node matrix
primeKG_nodes = primeKG_edges %>%
  .[, .(x_id, x_type, x_name, x_source)] %>%
  unique()
colnames(primeKG_nodes) = gsub("x", "node", colnames(primeKG_nodes))

# find and consolidate duplicate nodes
primeKG_nodes[, joint_id := paste(node_id, node_type, sep = "_")]
dup_list = primeKG_nodes[duplicated(joint_id), joint_id]
dup_nodes = primeKG_nodes %>%
  .[joint_id %in% dup_list] %>%

issues.title: PrimeKG vs Hetionet vs MSI
issues.body:
How does PrimeKG relate to [HetioNet](https://het.io/), which appears to be a smaller knowledge graph? For example, there might be variations in the definitions of diseases or in aggregation rules. HetioNet is also considered a state-of-the-art (SOTA) knowledge graph, yet it wasn’t discussed in the original paper.

Similarly, the Multiscale Interactome ([MSI](https://github.com/snap-stanford/multiscale-interactome)) was developed some time ago and also wasn’t included in the main paper, despite being co-developed by the author of PrimeKG.

Is it reasonable to assume that both HetioNet and MSI are substantially incorporated within PrimeKG, barring differences in ontology definitions?

issues.title: All edges seem to be duplicated in pre-build data files
issues.body:
When following the instructions as described in the `README.md` to load PrimeKG, the resulting dataframe has 8,100,498 edges/triples instead of the reported 4,050,249. 

So running 

```python
import pandas as pd
primekg = pd.read_csv('kg.csv', low_memory=False)
primekg.query('y_type=="disease"|x_type=="disease"')

print(len(primekg))
```
gives

```
8100498 rows × 10 columns
```

It seems that every triple appears twice in the data in source/target swapped form (what makes sense, given that the resulting dataframe contains exactly twice as many triples as reported in the `README.md`). The column swapped triples do not seem to have inverse relationships (what might cause problems in ML applications). In fact, the labels are identical, just the source and target columns are swapped.

Moreover, it looks like there exist 370 duplicates for the drug_protein label as can be seen by running 
```python
primekg[primekg.duplicated()]
``` 
what gives

```
370 rows × 10 c

__Updates:__

pull_requests.title: July 2023 update to PrimeKG
pull_requests.body:
Changes to rebuild PrimeKG and update the knowledge graph to include database releases up to July 2023. Note that 17 scripts `datasets/processing_scripts/` are re-run or updated to build a new version of PrimeKG, while `datasets/feature_construction/` scripts may be out-of-date. Re-run or updated primary data sources include Bgee, Comparative Toxicogenomics Database, DisGeNET, DrugBank, DrugCentral, NCBI Gene, Gene Ontology, Human Phenotype Ontology, MONDO, Reactome, SIDER, UBERON, and UMLS.

For more information, see `primary_data_resources.sh`. Changes include the following:

### General
Created script to automatically create directory structure, pull data, and run all necessary processing and feature extraction steps.
* Fixed broken environment construction script.
* Script automatically creates required directories.
* Added commands to retrieve gene names, details, and NCBI ID to UniProt ID mapping from [www.genenames.org](http://www.genenames.org/), then output to `vocab/g

pull_requests.title: Fix pyproject after API PR
pull_requests.body: Fix missing bracket in `pyproject.toml` after merging in #7 (seemed easiest since PR was coming from a now read-only fork). Also move API-specific dependencies to a separate, optional dependency group (`api`)

pull_requests.title: Fix updating DATA_DIR to current setting when loading checkpoint
pull_requests.body:
When loading a pretrained checkpoint, the serialized `ModelArgs` contain paths that use the `DATA_DIR` setting as it was when the model was trained. We need to update these to the current setting for the user loading the model.

The previous code for doing this updating was flawed, but happened to work in a few specific settings. This change switches to a more robust method of updating the data directory.

pull_requests.title: Add scripts and notebooks for updated experiments
pull_requests.body: Various scripts and notebooks associated with updated experiments. Update paper analysis README to match new figure panels, update main README

pull_requests.title: Add notebook for embedding figure
pull_requests.body: Add the notebook for recreating the embedding comparison plot in Figure 3d of the manuscript. Also includes some associated changes to add `scanpy` to the dependencies, add a dict of the canonical split names for some datasets, and remove an empty README.

pull_requests.title: Add notebook for reproducing analysis of retrieval with compositional prompts
Add additional notebooks for reproducing analyses
pull_requests.body:
Adds notebooks for reproducing:
- Parkinson's control gene lists
- Stability of protein retrieval across different methods for gene-disease associations

pull_requests.title: gene retrieval via API
pull_requests.body:
Copy code from protein retrieval example notebook into importable methods.

Wrap methods in fastapi app

retrieval can also be performed from new script that uses the same methods

pull_requests.title: Add notebook for STING example
pull_requests.body:
Adds the notebook for reproducing the STING1 retrieval analysis in Figure 3g of the manuscript. Also pulls out the description perturbation functions from `retrieval.ipynb` and into `inference_utils.py` so they can be shared across the two notebooks.

Control genes CSV for STING1 analysis was separately uploaded to ProCyon-Instruct.

pull_requests.title: Add notebook for recreating bertscore by QA filter thresh analysis
pull_requests.body: Reproducibility code for Extended Data Figure 8.

pull_requests.title: Add scripts and documentation for evaluating external LLM captions with reference-based metrics
pull_requests.body: Rename the `llm_judge_phenotype_eval` directory to `external_llm_eval` and add scripts/README section describing generation of reference-based metrics (i.e. BERTScore, ROUGE, BLEU) for external LLM phenotype generations.

pull_requests.title: Add scripts and documentation for external LLM evaluation
pull_requests.body:
Adds documentation and scripts to enable reproducibility of external LLM evaluations. For high-level documentation, see the README file at `examples/paper_analyses/llm_judge_phenotype_eval/README.md`. 

The added scripts can either be used to run a complete LLM comparison using the LLM-as-a-Judge approach starting from scratch, or if one wants to directly reproduce the results from the paper, the final notebook `parse_llm_judge_results.ipynb` can be used in conjunction with the original judge responses, which are contained in the ProCyon-Instruct dataset [here](https://huggingface.co/datasets/mims-harvard/ProCyon-Instruct/tree/main/experimental_data/llm_judge_eval/judge_responses).

pull_requests.title: Add evaluation framework documentation and scripts
pull_requests.body:
Add documentation for the evaluation framework and demo scripts.
- General cleanup of `framework` code and removal of older evaluation code
- Add example configs (datasets, models, eval args)
- Add README specific to the evaluation framework
- Update requirements and top-level README

pull_requests.title: Update dependencies, remove unused imports
pull_requests.body:
Updates `pyproject.toml` with additional needed dependencies. Install for `flash-attn` mirrors the guidelines given [here](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/concepts/projects/config/#build-isolation).

Also remove imports from dependencies that are no longer needed to reduce the number of installed packages.

Small changes to support loading pickled args classes with updated definitions.

Tested by making a new environment with provided installation instructions only and then running `retrieval.ipynb` with ProCyon-Full and ProCyon-Split models. Confirmed that ProCyon-Bind can be loaded.

issues.body: Hi, Can you provide pre-processed Boiler Dataset, It seems that link is not pre-processed version.

issues.title: A serious problem: the target domain label is used in the training phase.
issues.body:
From the code "def preprocess_labels(self, source_loader, target_loader):
trg_y = copy.deepcopy(target_loader.dataset.y_data)
src_y = source_loader.dataset.y_data
pri_c = np.setdiff1d(trg_y, src_y) # Get the private class in the target domain
mask = np.isin(trg_y, pri_c) # Mark the private sample in the target domain
trg_y[mask] = -1 # Mark the label of the private sample as -1
return trg_y, pri_c" we can see that

1. The true label trg_y of the target domain sample is directly extracted from target_loader.dataset.y_data, and the private class label set in the target domain is clearly obtained.
2. trg_y[mask] = -1, all "unknown classes" are marked as "-1", but there is more than one "unknown class" in the target domain, how to solve this problem?
3. The "unknown class" in the target domain is only detected by the bimodal, but how can the model recognize it? There is no relevant content for learning "unknown classes" in the code.

issues.title: Questions about the correct module
issues.body:
In the correct function in `algorithms/RAINCOAT.py`, `src_y` in the incoming arguments is not called, and the correct process is no different from the update training process.
```
    def correct(self, src_x, src_y, trg_x):
        self.coptimizer.zero_grad()
        src_feat, out_s = self.feature_extractor(src_x)
        trg_feat, out_t = self.feature_extractor(trg_x)
        src_recon = self.decoder(src_feat, out_s)
        trg_recon = self.decoder(trg_feat, out_t)
        recons = 1e-4 * (self.recons(trg_recon, trg_x) + self.recons(src_recon, src_x))
        recons.backward()
        self.coptimizer.step()
        return {'recon': recons.item()}
```
I'm not quite sure how this code achieves pulling close the same labeled samples and rejecting unknown samples in the target domain

issues.title: Is it fair to iterate 50 epochs separately in Alignment and Correction stages?
issues.body: Hi, I have some questions about Section "C.2. Experimental Details", in which you provide the key hyperparameters of the RAINCOAT algorithm. I noticed that in Table 5, 6, 7, and 8, the epoch for RAINCOAT and all comparison algorithms is 50. I found that in your code, you set the epoch of the Alignment and Correction stages of RAINCOAT to 50, which corresponds to E1=50 and E2=50 in the paper. This seems unfair because all comparison algorithms only iterate 50 epochs, while RAINCOAT iterates a total of 50+50=100 epochs. To ensure fairness, should E1+E2=50 be met?

issues.title: Some Question about Frequency Domain
issues.body:
Great Work on the Time series enhanced by frequency information🤩! I noticed that you did not use `ifft` to change back in time domain, which makes me curious about the reason, it is the first time I saw people directly use the amplitude and phrase. 
As the paper mentioned:
> "RAINCOAT extracts the polar coordinates of frequency coefficients to keep both **low-level (ai) and high-level (pi)** semantics. The frequency space features eF is a concatenation [ai; pi]."

Well, I am a student now beginning to focus on Time series OOD. I want to know the low-level (ai) and high-level (pi) semantics information you mentioned means what kind of information in time series. Thesedays, I did some experiments on data augmentations for OOD Domain generalization, and I noticed that low-frequency is more important than high-frequency. I want to explore whether there are some common invariants behind the frequency domain, so the amplitude and phrase are naturally to be considered.
I think there are

Best regards.

issues.title: Incorrect Implementation of DIRT-T ?
issues.body:
First let me thank you for your amazing contribution. As stated in the title I see some incoherences between your DIRT-T implementation and the original paper.  Your implementation looks more like VADA, introduced in the same paper and which also serve as an initialization method for DIRT-T.

The authors describe DIRT-T as "a recursive extension of VADA, where the act of pseudo-labeling of the target distribution constructs a new 'source' domain". The problem is that I don't see neither the recursivity nor the pseudo-labelization. The classifier h_(n-1) is supposed to serve as teacher for h_n with h_0, the initial classifier, being the one produced by VADA.

I might have not understood your code well, if it is the case sorry for the inconvenience.

Best regards,

issues.title: remove equals sign from filename in add_spl_to_patients.py
issues.body:
I noticed that the script add_spl_to_patients.py generates filenames that include an equals sign (=). 

https://github.com/mims-harvard/SHEPHERD/blob/e61281f0e6ee3617d1a889d90a9908454ba31e6f/data_prep/shortest_paths/add_spl_to_patients.py#L141

'mydata_agg=mean_spl_matrix.npy'

While this is technically allowed, I recommend avoiding this character in filenames. There are a few reasons for this, such as cross-platform compatibility, shell and command line confusion, potential for automation conflicts, and readability.

Recommendation: Consider replacing the equals sign with a more neutral character, such as an underscore (_) or a dash (-).

issues.title: NeighborSampler duplicates batch size - implementation question
issues.body:
Hello :) 

In the NeighborSampler in the sample method, the batch initialize with the following code:

```
# sample nodes to form positive edges. we will try to predict these edges
row, col, e_id = self.adj_t_sample.coo()
# NOTE: only does self loops when no edges in the current partition of the dataset
target_batch = random_walk(row, col, source_batch, walk_length=1, coalesced=False)[:, 1]
batch = torch.cat([source_batch, target_batch], dim=0)


batch_size: int = len(batch)
        adjs = []
        n_id = batch
        for size in self.sizes:
            adj_t, n_id = self.adj_t.sample_adj(n_id, size, replace=False)
.....
```
For an isolated node u, the random_walk returns u in a walk of length 1, but for other nodes the walk will return a random neighbor. 
Either way, the new batch, which now consist of duplicate amount of nodes, is then fed to the adj sampler. Hereby, sampling twice from the same nodes, or sampling neighbors from nodes that didn't appeared in th

pull_requests.body: added an optional data_path parameter that can be used to specify path where the spectra splits can be saved to. issues.title: harmonize_affinities discards data from BindingDB
issues.body:
**Describe the bug**
I noticed that for BindingDB the raw dataset contains 884846 unique SMILES-Protein sequence pairs and after applying `harmonize_affinities`, it contains only 766817 unique pairs.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
```
from tdc.multi_pred import DTI
loader_BindingDB_IC50_max = DTI(name = 'BindingDB_IC50')
data_IC50_max = loader_BindingDB_IC50_max.harmonize_affinities(mode = 'max_affinity')

loader_BindingDB_IC50_raw = DTI(name = 'BindingDB_IC50')
data_IC50_raw = loader_BindingDB_IC50_raw.get_data()

data_IC50_max.groupby(['Drug', 'Target']).count().shape
data_IC50_raw.groupby(['Drug', 'Target']).count().shape
```

**Expected behavior**
I would expect the same number of unique protein-molecule pairs after applying aggregation function. 

**Screenshots**
If applicable, add screenshots to help explain your problem.

**Environment:**
- OS: Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS (GNU/Linux 6.8.0-59-generic x86_64)
- Python version: Python 3.8.12
- TDC version: Version: 0.3.7

issues.title: BBB Martins Test Split labels
issues.body:
**Title:** Possible label errors in BBB_Martins test set — 2 molecules appear mislabeled

**Description:** While auditing the BBB_Martins dataset from the tdc.single_pred.ADME module, we identified two molecules in the test split that appear to have incorrect labels. These discrepancies affect evaluation metrics and introduce a ceiling on achievable accuracy (e.g., 99.51% max if 2/406 are mislabelled).

from tdc.single_pred import ADME
data = ADME(name = 'BBB_Martins')
split = data.get_split()

Thank you for your time.

issues.title: New DTI Dataset || Cache Challenge
issues.body:
Hey,

There's a DTI challenge website called [cache-challenge](https://cache-challenge.org/critical-assessment-computational-hit-finding-experiments) and at least for challenges 1-4, and was wondering if it would be of interest to integrate them? These are fairly difficult challenges for prediction models and have the added bonus of not having overlapping data with common training sets like bindingdb or pdbbind.

They do provide only a relatively small amount of data, but this dataset would act more as a test/validation set to challenge a prediction model.

issues.title: PPBR_AZ Benchmark and dataset mismatch
issues.body:
**Describe the bug**

The `PPBR_AZ` dataset returns a different number of molecules depending whether it is loaded via the `admet_group` benchmark or the `ADME` module. Additionally, the data loaded through `admet_group` does not match documentation.

- `PPBR_AZ` from` ADME` has 1614 molecules (train - 1130, valid - 161, test - 323).
- According to [`admet_group` documentation](https://tdcommons.ai/benchmark/admet_group/08ppbr/) `PPBR_AZ` should contain 1797 molecules in total.
- When loaded via `admed_group`, the dataset contains 2790 molecules (2231 train_valid, 559 test).

**To Reproduce**
```python
from tdc.benchmark_group import admet_group

group = admet_group(path="data/")
benchmark = group.get('PPBR_AZ')
benchmark['train_val'] # <-- 2231 rows dataframe
benchmark['test'] # <-- 559 rows, including duplicated molecules with different binding rates

from tdc.single_pred import ADME
data = ADME(name='PPBR_AZ')

train_split = data.get_split()['train'] # <-- 1130 molecules
valid_split

issues.title: license management
issues.body:
Another area of concern relates to the redistribution and usage of pre-trained models and datasets through the PyTDC platform. Given the diverse licensing terms associated with pre-trained models (some under open-source licenses, others with academic or non-commercial restrictions), how does PyTDC plan to:

    Clearly surface licensing terms and permissible use when users retrieve models especially through python editors?
    Ensure that models with restrictive licensing (e.g., non-commercial, academic use only) are not inadvertently used in ways that violate their original licenses?

Including a transparent licensing and provenance management layer would greatly enhance trust, reproducibility, and adoption in both academic and commercial settings, particularly for users in regulated industries like pharmaceutical research.

issues.title: diversity in sbdd
issues.body:
For the structure-based drug design component of PyTDC, beyond the scoring functions already included, there are two recent popular aspects to be considered in evaluating the molecules:

    The physical plausibility of 3D structures, such as PoseCheck [1].
    The diversity of generated molecules, new metrics including NCircles [2] and HamDiv [3].

The SBDD component may not be the main contribution of PyTDC, but as a comprehensive benchmarking platform, I believe these metrics would help.

[1] Harris C, Didi K, Jamasb A, et al. PoseCheck: Generative Models for 3D Structure-based Drug Design Produce Unrealistic Poses[C]//NeurIPS 2023 Generative AI and Biology (GenBio) Workshop.

[2] Xie Y, Xu Z, Ma J, et al. How Much Space Has Been Explored? Measuring the Chemical Space Covered by Databases and Machine-Generated Molecules[C]//The Eleventh International Conference on Learning Representations.

[3] Hu X, Liu G, Yao Q, et al. Hamiltonian diversity: effectively measuring molecular diversi

pull_requests.title: Replace rdkit.six with six
pull_requests.body:
This PR replaces every instance of `rdkit.six` functions with the `six` equivalent. This adds a `six` dependency, but makes TDC compatible with more recent versions of RDKit.

This fixes #374.

As a side note, completely migrating to Python 3 (and removing the `six` dependency) should be trivial; for instance, `pickle` works as a replacement for `six.cPickle` and instances of `six.iteritems(fps)` can be replaced by `fps.items()`.

pull_requests.title: move tests out of the package and use native `pytest` CLI
pull_requests.body:
#### Summary

This PR refactors our test execution strategy by:
- Moving test files out of the main package directory.
- Running tests directly via the `pytest` CLI instead of through a custom Python script.

#### Motivation

This change brings several benefits:

✅ **Cleaner Project Structure**  
Separating test files from the main package helps keep the codebase clean and maintainable. It reinforces a clear distinction between production code and test code.

✅ **Standardized Test Execution**  
Using `pytest` directly is the standard in the Python ecosystem. It simplifies the testing process, aligns us with community best practices, and improves compatibility with tooling (like IDEs, CI/CD systems, and coverage tools).

✅ **Better Developer Experience**  
Running tests becomes as simple as:
```bash
pytest tests/
```
No need to remember or maintain a custom script.

✅ Improved Maintainability
As our test suite grows, leveraging pytest’s powerful features (like fi

pull_requests.title: install this package for testing
pull_requests.body:
### Description
This PR removes the additional Conda environment file that was previously used for CI setup. Instead, the CI now installs the package directly, relying on its declared dependencies.

### Why this is beneficial

- **Single Source of Truth**: Ensures that tests run against the same dependencies declared by the package itself.
- **Reduced Maintenance**: No need to maintain a separate environment file in sync with the package dependencies.
- **Simpler CI Workflow**: Streamlines the setup process by reducing duplication and potential drift between test and runtime environments.

pull_requests.title: bump: relax `transformers` requirements
pull_requests.body:
## Update Transformers Version to Ensure Compatibility

The previously pinned Transformers version caused integration issues by conflicting with the project’s dependency requirements, resulting in errors due to non-overlapping version constraints. This PR updates the version constraint to allow compatibility with a broader range of dependencies while ensuring continued functionality. By updating to Transformers 4.50.2, this change improves flexibility, reduces dependency conflicts, and streamlines integration within the project.

additional to #354
cc: @amva13

pull_requests.title: split testing and linter
pull_requests.body:
This PR combines the linter and split testing into a single CI job, allowing them to run in parallel. While this won’t significantly reduce overall runtime, it helps keep the build job focused on testing without linter-related noise. Additionally, we can use [pre-commit.ci](https://github.com/marketplace/pre-commit-ci) to handle automated linting fixes, reducing manual effort and making the development process smoother.

By running both steps together, we maintain efficiency while keeping the pipeline clean and organized. Let me know if you have any thoughts or concerns!

it will help to validate/debug #358
cc: @amva13

pull_requests.title: bump: relax `sklearn` requirements
pull_requests.body:
## Update Scikit-Learn Version to Resolve Integration Conflicts

The previously pinned Scikit-Learn version caused integration issues by conflicting with the project’s dependency requirements, resulting in errors due to non-overlapping version constraints. This PR updates the version constraint to allow compatibility with a broader range of dependencies while ensuring continued functionality. This change improves flexibility, reduces dependency conflicts, and streamlines integration within the project.

Resolves https://github.com/mims-harvard/TDC/issues/354

pull_requests.title: pd.append -> pd.concat
pull_requests.body:
**Problem:** Pandas `append` has been deprecated since version 1.4 and has been removed since version 1.6. `create_group_split` still uses `pandas.append` to split the train_val set into the train and val sets.

**Solution:** Updates `create_group_split` to utilize `pandas.concat` to create the train and val sets.

pull_requests.title: Add node names instead of node IDs to networkx graph
pull_requests.body:
**Problem:** Each node id (`x_id` and `y_id`) is node type specific. For example, a node ID of "1" for a disease node is different from a node ID of "1" for a gene/protein node. This is (likely) not an issue for training a GNN. However, it can be an issue if users want to do network analysis on PrimeKG because the current `to_nx()` function does not consider node type.

**Solution:** Instead of adding pairs of node IDs as edges, add pairs of node names because they are unique.

issues.title: Accuracy metrics are generally not computed at the right time - attempted corrected code provided (trainer.py)
issues.body:
As I am attempting to go through the codebase, I noticed that both the finetuning and testing accuracy metrics are not computed correctly

There are couple main issue
1. In the trainer.py, the "best performance" metrics are reported for the highest test set accuracy across all fine tuning epochs. Due to the absence of a separate hold out test set, this may fall in the lines of cherry picking. 
2. In both fine tuning and testing, the accuracy metrics are computed in each mini batch, and then averaged at the end of the epoch to report. This affects accuracy, aucpr, and AUC. They should be computed across all data, not computed on mini-batch and then aggregated. If your dataset is imbalanced, I noticed that the AUCPR and AUC would always be around +- 0.05 around the random baseline of 0.5. I switched to only compute at the end across the entire epoch, and got 0.17 -0.18 
3. The binarized accuracy metrics (accuracy, recall, precision) are computed using fixed cutoff of 0.5. This might

issues.title: Tool call in Claude Code crashes when ToolUniverse tool name is > 45 characters long
issues.body:
### Description

The MCP standard specifies that tool names must be maximum 64 characters long [(link)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/community/seps/986-specify-format-for-tool-names#specification). When the tool name is > 64 characters long, the tool call fails and puts Claude Code in an error loop that requires to exit or clear the session context.

Claude Code tool names are constructed using this template `mcp__{mcp_server_name}__{tool_name}`. Here the prefix is `mcp__tooluniverse__` so this leaves only 45 characters maximum for the tool name defined in ToolUniverse.

97 tools are affected (see file `list_of_tools_affected_by_64_chr_limit.txt`).

N.B. I didn't test with OpenAI Codex but it's likely that a similar problem occurs.

### Example

```
>  Get FDA info on conditions for doctor consultation for marfan syndrome

. ToolSearch(Search tools: "select:mcp__tooluniverse__FDA_get_info_on_conditions_for_doctor_consultation_by_drug_name")
  -  Found 1 tool
  -  API Error: 400 {"ty

issues.title: Bug Report: OLS tool `ols_get_term_ancestors` returns empty terms array despite total_items > 0
issues.body:
**Description**
The `ols_get_term_ancestors` [tool from OLS](https://zitniklab.hms.harvard.edu/ToolUniverse/tools/ols_tools.html#ols-get-term-ancestors-type-olstool) (and perhaps others) returns an empty terms array even when `total_items` indicates ancestors exist. This appears to be caused by incorrect IRI encoding when calling the OLS4 API.

**Steps to Reproduce**

```python
from tooluniverse import ToolUniverse

tu = ToolUniverse()
tu.load_tools()

result = tu.run({
    'name': 'ols_get_term_ancestors',
    'arguments': {
        'operation': 'get_term_ancestors',
        'term_iri': 'http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/CL_0000084',
        'ontology': 'cl',
        'size': 50
    }
})

print(result)
```

**Actual Result**

```python
{
    'terms': [],  # Empty!
    'total_items': 15,  # But 15 items exist
    'showing': 0,
    'term_iri': 'http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/CL_0000084',
    'ontology': 'cl',
    'filters': {'include_obsolete': False}
}
```

**Expected Result**
The terms ar

issues.title: Integration into the Docker MCP Catalog
issues.body:
I think it would be very helpful to integrate ToolUniverse with the official Docker MCP (Moby Contract Protocol) Catalog. This is a new registry from Docker ([https://hub.docker.com/mcp/explore](https://hub.docker.com/mcp/explore)) designed for discovering and running containerized tools and servers directly within Docker Desktop. By packaging ToolUniverse as a "Local MCP Server," we could significantly boost its discoverability and ease of use for a massive audience of developers. This would make ToolUniverse available to users with a simple "enable" click from the MCP Toolkit, removing the need for manual cloning, building, and running. This seems like a high-value way to standardize access to the project and grow its user base.

I'm happy to help start the work on the required server.yaml configuration if the maintainers are open to this direction.

[https://github.com/docker/mcp-registry/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/docker/mcp-registry/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md)

issues.title: OpenAlexTool double-encoding search query, reducing results
issues.body:
File: `tooluniverse/openalex_tool.py`
Function: `OpenAlexTool.search_literature`, line 51

The tool incorrectly does:
```py
encoded_keywords = urllib.parse.quote(search_keywords)
params = {"search":encoded_keywords}
```

But `requests` automatically encodes query parameters.

This results in double encoding. With the example from the documentation:

"artificial intelligence" → "artificial%20intelligence" → "%2520"

Tested via:
```py
result = tu.run({
    "name": "openalex_literature_search",
    "arguments": {
        "search_keywords": "artificial intelligence",
        "max_results": 30
    }
})
```

**Expected**: OpenAlex returns 30 results
**Actual**: OpenAlex returns only 1 result

OpenAlex API confirms correct behavior:
https://api.openalex.org/works?search=artificial%20intelligence&per-page=30

Returns 30 results

Fix:
Remove manual encoding:
```py
params = { 
  "search": search_keywords,
  ...
}
```

issues.title: Can ToolUniverse support a fully local LLM deployment (instead of remote API calls)?
issues.body:
Hello ToolUniverse team,

I’m currently building a  AI agent using the ToolUniverse framework together with Qwen Code as the LLM backend.
By default, Qwen Code connects to the cloud using qwen-oauth (remote API calls).
However, for medical and research environments, we need to run everything locally — including the LLM — for data privacy and compliance reasons.
I would like to confirm the following:
1.Can ToolUniverse work with a locally deployed LLM (e.g., Qwen2.5-Coder-7B running on GPU) instead of a remote API model?
2. What configuration changes are needed in the ~/.qwen/settings.json (or equivalent) to make ToolUniverse connect to a local model rather than using OAuth / API endpoints?
3.Does ToolUniverse automatically detect the local inference backend (like vLLM, lmdeploy, or transformers), or should it be manually specified?
4.Is there an example or documentation showing how to set up ToolUniverse + local LLM for a fully offline workflow?

pull_requests.title: Add ESM-C as an MCP-native remote tool
pull_requests.body:
This PR adds **ESM-C (Evolutionary Scale Modeling)** as a **remote, MCP-native tool** in ToolUniverse, following the recommended *remote tool* onboarding pattern for models without an existing public API.

**Model**
ESM-C (from EvolutionaryScale / CZI Biohub)
GitHub: [https://github.com/evolutionaryscale/esm](https://github.com/evolutionaryscale/esm)

### What this PR does

* Implements a lightweight **FastMCP server** that wraps ESM-C inference and exposes a single MCP tool:

  * **`esm_embed_sequence`** — generates protein sequence embeddings using ESM-C.
* Adds an **`MCPAutoLoaderTool` configuration** (`esm_remote.json`) so ToolUniverse can automatically discover and invoke the tool via MCP.
* Keeps all model execution **fully remote**, with no ESM or GPU dependencies required on the ToolUniverse client side.

### Why this matters

* **Unblocks onboarding of Biohub / CZI models that do not yet have public APIs**, by providing a clean, agent-native interface layer.
*

pull_requests.title: Add NanoBio toxicity estimator tool (nanoparticle preclinical screening)
pull_requests.body:
## Summary
This PR adds a new NanoBio tool module under `src/tooluniverse/tools/nanobio/`:
- `nanobio_toxicity_estimator`: deterministic, early-stage nanoparticle toxicity risk estimation from size (nm), surface charge (mV), and material.

## Motivation
NanoBio tools aim to support agentic scientific workflows by providing structured, auditable utilities for nanomedicine research. This first tool is intended as a lightweight baseline for preclinical screening and educational/research use.

## Tool Details
- **Tool name:** `nanobio_toxicity_estimator`
- **Inputs:** `size_nm`, `charge_mV`, `material`
- **Outputs:** `toxicity_score` (0–10), `risk_level`, `reasons`, `confidence`, `success`
- **Deterministic:** yes (no randomness)

## Notes / Safety
This tool is intended for research and preclinical screening only and is not a clinical decision-making system.

## Author
Ghassan Muammar (Experts Group FZE)

pull_requests.body:
This PR completes the full EUHealth + Datastore pass from a user perspective.
---

## Summary of Key Additions

### **1. New user-facing CLI command: `tu-datastore add-tool`**

* Lets users install their own JSON tool definitions into:

  ```
  ~/.tooluniverse/data/user_tools/
  ```

* Enables automatic tool discovery by ToolUniverse and Codex for that user **without affecting the global ToolUniverse**
* This means that now users can make their own data agent searchable and their data and associated tools can easily be used in ToolUniverse (and Codex)
* Supports overwrite, custom names, and error-safe copying.

### **2. Automatic loading of user tools in `default_config.py`**

* Any tool JSON in `~/.tooluniverse/data/user_tools/` is now auto-detected.
* Makes custom datastores actually usable inside Codex without repository edits.
* This automatic loading only occurs in that user's local ToolUniverse - tested to make sure it does not impact global ToolUniverse

#

pull_requests.title: Add Dockerfile for Docker MCP Registry integration
pull_requests.body:
# Add Dockerfile for Docker MCP Registry Integration

Related to #44

This PR is **Step 1** of the Docker MCP Registry integration. After this is merged, Step 2 will be submitting the `server.yaml` configuration to the [docker/mcp-registry](https://github.com/docker/mcp-registry) repository.

This PR adds a Dockerfile to enable ToolUniverse integration with the [Docker MCP Registry](https://hub.docker.com/mcp/explore), making ToolUniverse discoverable and easily deployable through Docker Desktop's MCP Toolkit.

## Overview

The Dockerfile creates a containerized ToolUniverse MCP server that:

- Uses Python 3.12-slim as the base image
- Installs build dependencies (gcc, g++) for packages that require compilation (like `traits`)
- Installs runtime libraries (libxrender1, libxext6, libsm6, libx11-6, libexpat1) needed by RDKit
- Installs `tooluniverse` from PyPI (all dependencies are automatically handled via `pyproject.toml`)
- Removes build dependencies after installation

This PR updates the following files:

* `pyproject.toml`
* `.pre-commit-config.yaml`
* `.github/workflows/tests.yml`
* `uv.lock`

by removing the use of autoflake, flake8, and black. These tools are replaced with the use of Ruff instead. All other file changes are a result of executing `ruff check --fix` and `ruff format` on the repo.

pull_requests.title: Fix warning suppression
pull_requests.body:
Warning messages are not suppressed correctly and still show up when executing `tooluniverse-smcp` on the commandline.

This occurs because the warnings are not getting caught by the `warnings.catch_warnings()` context manager as they occur outside of that context. This PR fixes the problem by removing the context manager.

pull_requests.title: Make datastore & EUHealth plug-and-play: cache-dir defaults, auto-dim, personal HF sync, user-first docs
pull_requests.body:
This PR focuses on **user experience**: fewer knobs, safer defaults, clearer docs, and workflows that non-technical users can run end-to-end without touching code.

### Why this helps non-technical users

* **Zero setup paths:** Artifacts live in `<user_cache_dir>/embeddings/` by default, so people don’t have to pick or manage a project folder (and we avoid permission issues).
* **No “what’s my embedding dim?” questions:** We auto-detect with `get_model_dim(provider, model)` and record the true `embedding_model`/`embedding_dimensions`. CLI no longer asks for `--dim`.
* **Hugging Face “just works”:**

  * **Upload** defaults to the user’s own namespace (`<username>/<collection>`) from `HF_TOKEN`. (using get_user_cache_dir from utils.py)
  * **Download** uses a unified helper and lands files in the cache dir.
  * Tool JSONs can be uploaded and downloaded from/to HF (`--tool-json` on upload; `--include-tools` on download), so sharing a tool is easy. Before it was just the .db an

pull_requests.title: feature: add EMBL-EBI Ontology Lookup Service (OLS) tools
pull_requests.body:
Sort of an exploration into contributing tools from existing MCP servers.

The Ontology Lookup Service (OLS) is a repository for biomedical ontologies that aims to provide a single point of access to the latest ontology versions. This allows for standardization of biomedical metadata and provides a grounded mapping crucial for meta-analysis across different biomedical datasets.

My team at [CCB ](https://dbmi.hms.harvard.edu/about-dbmi/core-computational-biomedicine) has found moderate success at using [ols-mcp-server ](https://github.com/seandavi/ols-mcp-server)by Sean Davis with claude desktop. I converted the tool functions into a local tool in ToolUniverse. 

Another approach could be to simply use the [remote mcp server ](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ols4/api/mcp) but I think this is having hosting issues.

pull_requests.title: Add generalizable datastore and euhealth tool
update coding tools and tests system and vis
Update vllm local model support for agentic tools
feat: add Cellosaurus tools for cell line data access
pull_requests.body:
## Description

This PR adds Cellosaurus tools, a new tool suite for accessing the [Cellosaurus cell line database API](https://api.cellosaurus.org/api-methods). The tools provide functionality to:

- Search for cell lines using Solr query syntax
- Convert natural language queries to Solr syntax
- Retrieve detailed information about specific cell lines
- Handle API errors gracefully with meaningful error messages

## Changes Made

- ✅ **Tool Implementation**: Complete Cellosaurus tool suite with all required methods
- ✅ **Testing**: Unit tests with comprehensive coverage and API mocking
- ✅ **Documentation**: Comprehensive API docs and usage examples
- ✅ **Configuration**: Support for custom API endpoints (base URL)
- ✅ **Error Handling**: Robust error handling with detailed error messages

## Data Source Information

- **API**: Cellosaurus API v1.0
- **Documentation**: https://api.cellosaurus.org/api-fields
- **Rate Limits**: No official limits specified
- **Auth

pull_requests.title: Add OpenRouter support
pull_requests.body:
**Summary**
This PR adds comprehensive OpenRouter support to ToolUniverse, enabling access to 400+ models from multiple providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Qwen, and more) through a single unified API. This significantly expands the available LLM options for AI scientists without requiring separate API keys for each provider.

**Motivation**
Unified Access: Users can access models from OpenAI (GPT-5), Anthropic (Claude Sonnet 4.5), Google (Gemini 2.5), and other providers with a single API key
Cost Flexibility: OpenRouter provides competitive pricing and pay-as-you-go billing across providers
Simplified Configuration: Reduces the complexity of managing multiple API keys and client implementations
Enhanced Fallback: Provides additional fallback options when primary APIs are unavailable

pull_requests.title: Add Generalizable Datastore: Hybrid Search (Keyword + Embeddings), CLI, and Tool Support as well as UHealth Tools: Topic Search + Deep-Dive with Weekly Refreshed Datastore
pull_requests.body:
I have split up this PR description into a PR#1 **generalizable datastore work** and a PR#2 for **EUHealth** tools. This is because the generalizable datastore work is applicable to anyone adding new tools and wanting to incorporate their data into ToolUniverse while EUHealth is a set of tools for ToolUniverse (21 tools).


---

# PR 1 — ToolUniverse Datastore: make your data searchable (keyword + embeddings + hybrid) and usable from tools

## Why

ToolUniverse users need to be able to answer the following questions: “how do I make my docs/spreadsheets searchable by meaning?” and “how do I plug that into tools?”. This PR adds a small, documented datastore that anyone can build in minutes and then query via CLI or from an Agent tool.

## What’s included

### New CLI

`tu-datastore` – one command with four subcommands:

* `build` – upsert a collection, insert docs (with de-dup), embed, write FAISS
* `search` – query by `keyword`, `embedding`, or `hybrid`
* `quickbuild`

issues.title: GPU requirement
issues.body:
What are the more demanding areas of the project in terms of GPU memory?
(Ie. Why the suggested 80gb?)

I'm going through the code trying to find it, but I'm not sure yet.

I've worked on my own agents that reduce GPU vram use by, for instance:
1. Processing content separately, reducing to potentially-related material only. 
2. When encountering content larger than the available model's context, it processes it in overlapping chunks, doing the same extraction as step 1 on each, then doing a subsequent pass to remove redundant information.
3. A final query is run on the concatenated (labeled) set of documents.

Is there any desire to make this attainable on consumer-grade GPU?  (I'm on a 24gb 3090 for instance).

pull_requests.title: TxGNN Restful API - FastAPI with latest CUDA & PyTorch Support and Docker Compose
pull_requests.body:
This PR provides an out-of-the-box the implementation of a RESTful API that exposes TxGNN's functionality for zero-shot therapeutic predictions and explanations using geometric deep learning (GNN). This API is deployed using **FastAPI**, with **CUDA** support for GPU acceleration, and containerized using **Docker**. Additionally, the repository includes a `Makefile`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `.env` file for convenient management and deployment of the application.

It all worked after pushing the stack to latest tech meaning:
CUDA: 12.4
PyTorch: 2.4.0
DGL: 2.4.0+cu124

## TL;DR - Quickstart

1. **Clone the repository**
    ```bash
    git clone https://github.com/healthecosystem/TxGNN
    cd TxGNN
    cp .env.example .env
    ```
2. Run the application using Make
    ```bash
    make run
    ```
3. Access the API Once the services are up, the API will be [locally accessible at port 8883](http://localhost:8883).

## Features
- **CUDA-enabled**: GPU-accelerated pr

pull_requests.title: Update pytorch, CUDA, dgl, torch-compile, Triton
pull_requests.body:
This is A WIP that updates the underlaying compute stack to ultimately port the models to moderrn pytorch architecture using `nn.Module`. This will enable to leverage torch.compile as well as using Triton / CUDA kernels down the line... 

Target versions:

PyTorch: 2.4.0
DGL: 2.4.0+cu124

issues.title: Does Decagon regard the relation and its corresponding reverse one as different relations?
issues.body:
Typically, DDI prediction is a pairwise classification problem. 
In the given toy example, you artificially generate a small graph.
 My concern is that Decagon seems to treat a DDI and its corresponding reverse one as two different edges. But, I am confused by how to calculate the metrics with the predicted results for potential DDI triplets and the reverse ones.
For instance, "Drug A's metabolism is increased when combined with Drug B" (symbolized as triplet `(A, metabolism increased, B)`) is semantically equal to the reverse one "Drug B can increase the metabolism of Drug A" (symbolized as triplet `(B, increase metabolism, A)`). 
https://github.com/mims-harvard/decagon/blob/86ff6b1423e548c22cbb8f70c5dac22b79d45290/main.py#L140-L145
If the model gives different scores for both triplets, how to calculate the final metric values. By simply keeping predicted results for both groups?

issues.title: data leakage problem in your model
issues.body:
The design of your adjacency matrix `adj_mats_orig` and the way you split the train/test set will cause a huge **data leakage problem** in your training, because your validation and test set is created independently for `gene_adj` and `gene_adj.transpose(copy=True)`, and therefore the edges from the validation / test set in `gene_adj` is actually included in the training set of `gene_adj.transpose(copy=True)`.

Same problem goes for the train / validate set between `gene_drug_adj` and `drug_gene_adj`. The validation edges from `gene_drug_adj` are actually used for training  in `drug_gene_adj`, and vise versa. 

Could you please clarify?
Thanks!

_Originally posted by @hurleyLi in https://github.com/marinkaz/decagon/issues/7#issuecomment-519645774_

TensorFlow 2.5.0

Release 2.5.0

Major Features and Improvements

  • Support for Python3.9 has been added.
  • tf.data:
    • tf.data service now supports strict round-robin reads, which is useful for synchronous training workloads where example sizes vary. With strict round robin reads, users can guarantee that consumers get similar-sized examples in the same step.
    • tf.data service now supports optional compression. Previously data would always be compressed, but now you can disable compression by passing compression=None to tf.data.experimental.service.distribute(...).
    • tf.data.Dataset.batch() now supports num_p

pull_requests.body:
Bumps [bleach](https://github.com/mozilla/bleach) from 1.5.0 to 3.1.4.
Changelog

Sourced from bleach's changelog.

Version 3.1.4 (March 24th, 2020)

Security fixes

  • bleach.clean behavior parsing style attributes could result in a regular expression denial of service (ReDoS).

    Calls to bleach.clean with an allowed tag with an allowed style attribute were vulnerable to ReDoS. For example, bleach.clean(..., attributes={'a': ['style']}).

    This issue was confirmed in Bleach versions v3.1.3, v3.1.2, v3.1.1, v3.1.0, v3.0.0, v2.1.4, and v2.1.3. Earlier versions used a similar regular expression and should be considered vulnerable too.

    Anyone using Bleach <=v3.1.3 is encouraged to upgrade.

pull_requests.title: Adding 06-GNNExplainer Tutorial
pull_requests.body: I created a separate folder for the 6th tutorial on GNNExplainer. This folder contains the jupyter notebook containing the tutorial, some utility functions, a reference table for dealing with GO term annotations and pretrained parameters for running the tutorial without a GPU training required.

CI flow setup, updated installation and environment.yml
pull_requests.body:
- Introduced script `install.sh` which should allow for installation on cross platform devices.
- Removed unused dependencies in `environment.yml`, alongside updating to `python 3.8.5`
- Added github action for checking dependencies (`.test/`) on pull requests (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`) to `master`

pull_requests.title: Add GAT notebook
pull_requests.body: This notebook shows how to implement GAT from scratch, and then in torch_geometric. Doing so shows the user what torch_geometric actually does, and familiarizes them with the graph attention mechanism.

pull_requests.title: Add notebook for simple link-prediction task
pull_requests.body:
The notebook demonstrates how to obtain node embeddings on an undirected graph. We use a small graph of drug-drug interactions obtained from [SNAP biodata](http://snap.stanford.edu/biodata/), using the ChCh-Miner graph. The notebook provides minimal examples of fitting a simple GCN autoencoder using batch learning, as well as graphSAINT.

I wrote the notebook in the same style as the other notebooks in the repo. Comments/corrections are welcome, as this is something I am still learning these techniques.
